parsed.justification: The cycle is: unemployment -> sp500_ret -> vix -> ig_spread -> unemployment. To break it, I need to remove or reverse one edge. Examining the four edges: (1) unemployment -> sp500_ret: rising unemployment weakens earnings expectations and weighs on equity returns — plausible but low consensus. (2) sp500_ret -> vix: large negative equity returns mechanically drive VIX higher through the leverage effect and put-option demand — this is one of the most well-documented mechanical relationships in finance (VIX is literally derived from S&P 500 option prices), so reversing or removing it would be causally wrong. (3) vix -> ig_spread: elevated market-wide fear (VIX) leads investors to demand higher compensation for corporate credit risk, widening IG spreads — this is a well-established transmission channel from equity volatility to credit markets and should be retained in this direction. (4) ig_spread -> unemployment: the proposed direction is that credit spread widening causes unemployment to rise. While there is a real channel (tighter credit conditions reduce corporate investment and hiring), the more defensible causal sequence runs in reverse over short horizons: rising unemployment (deteriorating economic fundamentals) signals recession risk, which causes investors to demand higher credit risk premiums, widening IG spreads. Unemployment is a coincident/lagging real-economy indicator that credit markets price in prospectively — credit spreads are forward-looking financial prices that anticipate labor market deterioration rather than respond to it. Reversing ig_spread -> unemployment to unemployment -> ig_spread means: rising unemployment (worsening macro fundamentals) causes credit spreads to widen, which is the more causally defensible direction. This also cleanly breaks the cycle while preserving the other three causally well-grounded edges in their current directions.
